ocr.text: The analysis of predictors of well-being is essential for understanding the factors that contribute to individuals' overall quality
of life. In the accompanying table, several key metrics are presented, including the coefficients (b) and standard errors (SE) for
each predictor variable examined. These metrics provide insights into the strength and significance of the relationships between
various predictors and overall well-being. For instance, the statistical significance of predictors can be assessed through the t-
values and associated p-values, allowing researchers to identify which factors are most influential in enhancing well-being

outcomes. 

Moreover, the effect size, represented by the f² metric, offers a quantitative measure of how substantially each
predictor contributes to the model, thereby indicating the practical relevance of these findings.
Predicted  Predictor  b  SE  DF  t  p  f2
PREDICTORS OF WELL-BEING
1  Well-being  Thinking about the past  −0.03  0.01  250.83  −2.73  0.01  0.02
2  Well-being  Thinking about the future  −0.03  0.01  250.97  −2.08  0.04  0.01
PREDICTORS OF RUMINATION
4  Rumination  Thinking about the past  0.41  0.04  252.89  10.34  <0.001  0.40
5  Rumination  Thinking about the future  0.09  0.05  252.50  1.70  0.09  0.01
MEDIATION MODELS
6  Well-being  Thinking about the past  0.00  0.01  247.77  0.17  0.87
8  Well-being  Thinking about the future  −0.02  0.01  250.30  −1.66  0.10
Understanding these relationships is critical for policymakers and practitioners aiming to design interventions that promote
well-being across different demographics. By analyzing the data presented in the table, stakeholders can make informed
decisions grounded in empirical evidence, ultimately leading to more effective strategies for improving individual and
community well-being. As such, the insights derived from this analysis underscore the importance of targeted approaches in
fostering a healthier, more fulfilling life for individuals.
